# Transcrição: 4. What Industry's and People Should Make Cold Calls

- **Fonte:** 4. What Industry's and People Should Make Cold Calls
- **Duração:** 13:02

## [00:00 - 03:00]

So what industries and people should make cold calls? I guess a better question would be who should not make them? Alright, who would not be in a position? This would be the better question. Who would start a company or a business or be an entrepreneur? Or you're out there, you're a 17-year-old person and you're trying to build your brand up. Who's not going to have to make this call one day, some day to someone? You're a startup. You're going to have to make a cold call to somebody. I mean at some point you've got to call somebody. You want to expand your company, you want to find that director of marketing, that VP of sales, the angel investor. At some point you're going to have to make a call to somebody. You want Google, Google is going to buy your company. At some point you're going to have to pick up the phone and make a call to the people at Google, right? What startup would not have to make a cold call at some point? Well I'm not doing that. I'm the startup guy. I'm the owner. I'm the entrepreneur. I'm the John Lamb with the big idea. Well good John, you've got to pick up the phone Mr. Lamb and call somebody because you don't want to make calls. You're going to have to call a team of people to bring in that are going to make the call. Sooner or later you're making a cold call. Sales organizations. I know sales organizations that are convinced that they shouldn't even make cold calls. It's the most whacked out thing that I've ever heard in my life. Like a sales organization says we don't call. We never ever do we call anybody that we don't know. Really? How are you going to grow your company if you don't bring in people that don't know about your company? Your product, your idea, your promotion, how good your deal is or how good your product is. You wouldn't call somebody? Your church needs money. Okay that is a sales proposition. Your church, your community church needs money to add an orphanage addition to the church. Somebody's got to call somebody and sell somebody on giving some money for this addition to the orphanage for the church, right? That's a sale. That's a cold call. Direct marketing. What about you guys in direct marketing? Multi-level marketing, direct marketing, direct sales. You going to call your mom up? Hey mom, mom, I'm going to a meeting tonight. Want to come with me? Right? Oh I can't do that. I can't impose myself on my mom. Job seekers, inventors, technology companies. How about agents trying to acquire the next big sport or entertainment or celebrity person? You think CAA doesn't have to make a cold call to somebody that's turned hot on fire? They're out of nowhere. They're celebrity now and they don't want to get some of that. landscapers, roofers, plumbers, car dealers, contractors? Who is not going to make a call? How about this? You're looking for a job. You want a job. I'm not even trying to sell something. I'm trying to buy something and I will have to make a cold call, right? Why? Because I want to make a lower cost of acquisition to create an opportunity for my company. Number two, I would make a cold call because I want to get lucky with a lead. I want to get lucky with a lead. I want to actually get lucky. I have a chance to get lucky with somebody that would be in the market. One of my first jobs was as a car salesman. I was 25 years old selling automobiles. I didn't have any business. I didn't know anybody. Nobody had ever bought a car from me. Are you serious, man? Are you serious? Bobby, I'm selling Toyotas, right? Boom, one of my first sales was to Bobby's mom. I got lucky with a lead. And then you know what happened? Well, then I'm going to have to make that call to Jared and say, Jared, John told me that you might be, right? So I'm going to have to call a referral. Find and call referrals. Number four, employees that want to break their people in. I'm an employer, okay? I employ people, right? So I want to break my people in. And the way I would break my people in is with cold calls. Here's our products. Here's who we are. Here's what we do. I want you to call people and tell them. Because what's a better way to work out your presentation to break people in about your company, to train your people who your company is, than to say, hey, I work for this company and present that to the public. ## [09:02 - 12:02]

that I even prospected him, that we even had a conversation. I walk in and say, hey, I called you the other day. I was in Miami, called you in San Antonio, told you I was coming down. Who's going to do this? Who's not going to have to do it at some point? Number three, let's say you're a software company. My third company or my second company was a software business. I had a software business and we sold that software to companies that wanted more processes that they could measure in their business. And we sold that to businesses. So we pick up the phone. When we were in monster expansion, we pick up the phone and call people rather than just go by and visit them and say, hey, can I show you a new product? My third company, and we still own, is a real estate company. In fact, this is my biggest business today, required a tremendous amount of cold calling. First, we had to call brokers for deals. Hey, do you have a deal? Do you know of a deal? Do you have any deals coming up? My name's Grant Cardone. That's a sales pitch, you understand? Very, very difficult to get a broker to really pay attention to me. I've perfected this particular call, all right? Do you have deals coming? I perfected that call and was able to fill my pipeline up with good deals. The first call that I had to make was to sellers to get deals because brokers get a little lazy, frankly, okay? They're not on their game all the time. They don't make cold calls. Of course, they shouldn't have to because they're selling real estate and they're so important. So I would bypass the broker and call sellers that they hadn't worked because I was trying to build a real estate company. If you want people to know you, you have to pick up this phone. So I call Gary Vaynerchuk and say, Gary, I want you on my program. I call Fran Tarkin. This is Grant Cardone. I never heard of you. This is a cold call to an NFL ballplayer. I'm trying to get talent on my show. We did that four weeks ago. He's coming on my show now. Okay? I'm going to have to use it to recruit. Cold calling is used to poach talent from other companies. Your company should have somebody in it that's poaching talent from my company. That's grabbing great people from me, and that's going to be a cold call because they're not calling you. ## [12:02 - 13:02]

The point of this is what? Who's not going to have to make a cold call? So the next time you hear or see some wacko saying cold calling is dead, consider this. I have four companies, and we're making hundreds of phone calls every day that would be considered cold. To find employees, to get paid, to get introduced, to get PR, right? To find product, to buy product, to deal with vendors, to collect money, you're going to make a cold call. Get used to it. Grow up, okay? Grow a set. Get your head on right. Say, hey, that phone is for me to reach out and touch someone. The fact that your mommy and daddy told you not to talk to strangers does not mean you don't need to talk to strangers. You have to learn how to make this cold call. So when you go back over this, I want you to look at how many different places. How many different places in your career right now should you be making cold calls and you're not?
